description_long: This guard stone consists of three large, upright, roughly rectangular stone blocks arranged in a trilithon formation, with two vertical stones supporting a horizontal lintel stone on top. The stones are weathered and display a mottled gray coloration with darker patches, suggesting exposure to the elements over time. The structure is set on a flat, circular patch of green grass, which serves as its base. The stones have irregular, naturalistic surfaces with visible textural roughness and some signs of minor chipping or erosion. The overall appearance is reminiscent of ancient megalithic monuments, such as those found at Stonehenge, and the stones are massive and imposing. There are no visible inscriptions or decorative markings on the stones, and the object is clearly intended as a robust, immovable barrier.
description: A massive trilithon-style guard stone made of three weathered gray stone blocks atop a grassy base, featuring rough, natural surfaces and no visible markings.
description_view_0: Front view showing the trilithon structure with two upright stones and a horizontal lintel, all set on a grassy base.
description_view_1: Side view revealing a single upright stone with its rough, irregular surface and mottled coloration.
description_view_2: Rear view similar to the front, displaying the trilithon arrangement with the horizontal stone and two vertical supports.
description_view_3: Opposite side view showing the other upright stone, again highlighting the stone's rough texture and natural form.
attribute_location: Typically found at the entrance to historical sites, parks, or as a protective barrier near urban street corners or driveways, often positioned to prevent vehicle access or demarcate boundaries.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, standing upright with the vertical stones embedded slightly into the earth for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible surface erosion, minor chipping at edges and corners, and some discoloration due to prolonged exposure to weather and possible vehicle or foot contact at the base. The top surfaces may show more smoothing from rainwater runoff.
